title: Altered Mental Status

sections.title: Definition & Spectrum
sections.content: Altered mental status (AMS) encompasses any change from baseline cognitive function, ranging from subtle confusion to deep coma. It's a symptom, not a diagnosis - always find the cause.
keyPoints: Spectrum: Confusion → Delirium → Lethargy → Obtundation → Stupor → Coma
Acute AMS is a medical emergency until proven otherwise
Always establish BASELINE - what was normal for this patient?
Common mimics: dementia, psychiatric illness, intoxication, post-ictal
Elderly may present with AMS as only sign of serious illness

sections.title: Immediate Assessment: ABCs + Glucose
sections.content: Before extensive workup, address immediately life-threatening causes.
keyPoints: Airway: Can they protect it? GCS ≤8 generally needs intubation
Breathing: Hypoxia and hypercarbia alter consciousness
Circulation: Hypotension = hypoperfusion of brain
Dextrose (or fingerstick glucose): Hypoglycemia is rapidly reversible
Disability: Pupils, focal deficits, signs of trauma
Don't Forget: Thiamine BEFORE glucose in suspected alcoholism (Wernicke's)

sections.title: The AEIOU-TIPS Mnemonic
sections.content: A systematic approach to the differential diagnosis of AMS.
keyPoints: A - Alcohol/Acidosis (intoxication, withdrawal, DKA, uremia)
E - Electrolytes/Endocrine/Encephalopathy (Na, Ca, glucose, thyroid, hepatic)
I - Infection (meningitis, encephalitis, UTI in elderly, sepsis)
O - Overdose/Oxygen (drugs, CO poisoning, hypoxia)
U - Uremia (renal failure)
T - Trauma/Temperature (head injury, hypothermia, hyperthermia)
I - Insulin (hypoglycemia, DKA/HHS)
P - Psychiatric/Poisoning (psychosis, serotonin syndrome, NMS)
S - Stroke/Seizure/Shock (CVA, status epilepticus, post-ictal, cardiogenic)

sections.title: History & Physical Pearls
sections.content: Key elements of history and exam that narrow the differential.

subsections.name: Critical History (from family, EMS, bystanders)
items: Timeline: Sudden (stroke, seizure, arrhythmia) vs gradual (metabolic, infection)
Medications: New meds, missed doses, access to pills
Substances: Alcohol, recreational drugs, withdrawal timeline
Medical history: Diabetes, liver disease, kidney disease, seizure disorder
Recent illness: Fever, URI, UTI symptoms
Trauma: Even minor - elderly may not recall falls

subsections.name: Physical Exam Focus
items: Vitals: Fever (infection, withdrawal, thyroid storm), hypothermia (sepsis, myxedema, environmental)
Pupils: Pinpoint (opioids, pontine stroke), dilated (sympathomimetics, anticholinergics)
Skin: Track marks, rash (meningococcemia), jaundice, dry vs diaphoretic
Neck: Stiffness (meningitis), thyroid (storm, myxedema)
Neuro: Focal deficits (stroke), asterixis (hepatic/uremic), seizure activity

sections.title: Diagnostic Workup
sections.content: A tiered approach based on clinical suspicion.

subsections.name: Tier 1: All Patients with AMS
items: Fingerstick glucose (at bedside IMMEDIATELY)
Basic metabolic panel (Na, K, Ca, glucose, BUN/Cr)
CBC (infection, anemia)
Urinalysis (UTI in elderly is common!)
Pulse oximetry and ABG/VBG if indicated

subsections.name: Tier 2: Based on Clinical Suspicion
items: CT Head: trauma, focal neuro signs, anticoagulation, new/severe headache
Lumbar puncture: fever + meningismus, immunocompromised, unexplained after CT
Liver function, ammonia: known liver disease, asterixis
TSH: myxedema coma, thyroid storm
Toxicology screen: suspected ingestion
Blood cultures: suspected sepsis
ECG: arrhythmia, ischemia

subsections.name: Tier 3: Specialized Testing
items: EEG: suspected non-convulsive status epilepticus
MRI brain: encephalitis, posterior reversible encephalopathy
Carboxyhemoglobin: CO exposure suspected
Cortisol: adrenal crisis
Osmolality + osmolar gap: toxic alcohol ingestion

sections.title: Common Treatable Causes - Don't Miss These
sections.content: Certain causes are rapidly reversible if recognized.
keyPoints: HYPOGLYCEMIA: Most common, most treatable - always check glucose first!
OPIOID OVERDOSE: Naloxone trial if pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression
WERNICKE'S: Give thiamine before glucose in alcoholics/malnourished
MENINGITIS: If suspected, give antibiotics BEFORE LP/CT
HYPERTENSIVE ENCEPHALOPATHY: BP >220/120 with neuro changes - lower BP carefully
HYPOXIA/HYPERCARBIA: Check ABG, supplemental O2
STATUS EPILEPTICUS: May be non-convulsive - low threshold for EEG

subsections.name: Elderly
items: UTI is #1 cause of AMS in elderly (even without urinary symptoms!)
Medications: polypharmacy, drug interactions, anticholinergic burden
Subdural hematoma: may occur without recalled trauma
Baseline dementia: family essential to determine change from baseline
Lower threshold for sepsis workup

subsections.name: Diabetics
items: Hypoglycemia vs DKA vs HHS
HHS can present with focal deficits mimicking stroke
Check glucose, electrolytes, osmolality, ketones

subsections.name: Known Psychiatric Illness
items: Never assume AMS is psychiatric without excluding medical causes
NMS: if on antipsychotics + fever + rigidity
Lithium toxicity: tremor, ataxia, AMS

keyTakeaways: AMS is a symptom - always find the underlying cause
Check glucose IMMEDIATELY at bedside - hypoglycemia is common and treatable
Give thiamine BEFORE glucose in alcoholics/malnourished
Use AEIOU-TIPS to systematically consider causes
UTI is the #1 cause in elderly - even without urinary symptoms
CT head not routine - get it for trauma, focal deficits, anticoagulation
If meningitis suspected, give antibiotics BEFORE imaging/LP
Non-convulsive status epilepticus is a hidden cause - consider EEG

reviewQuestions.question: A 78-year-old nursing home resident is brought in for 'acting confused.' Vitals: T 37.8°C, HR 92, BP 128/78. No focal deficits. What is the MOST likely cause?
reviewQuestions.explanation: UTI is the #1 cause of AMS in the elderly and may present without classic urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency). Low-grade fever in this population with confusion should prompt UA and urine culture. CT head is not first-line without focal deficits or trauma.

reviewQuestions.question: A disheveled man is found unresponsive with pinpoint pupils and RR of 6. What should you give first?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression = opioid overdose until proven otherwise. Naloxone 0.4-2mg IV/IM/IN can be diagnostic and therapeutic. Support respirations (bag-valve mask) while giving naloxone. May need repeated doses.

reviewQuestions.question: Before giving IV dextrose to an alcoholic patient with AMS, what should you give first?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Thiamine 100mg IV should be given before glucose in suspected alcoholics or malnourished patients. Glucose metabolism depletes thiamine; giving glucose to a thiamine-deficient patient can precipitate or worsen Wernicke's encephalopathy.

references: Kanich W, et al. Altered mental status: evaluation and etiology in the ED. Am J Emerg Med 2002
Han JH, et al. Delirium in the Emergency Department. Ann Emerg Med 2010
Xiao HY, et al. Altered Mental Status. Emerg Med Clin North Am 2016

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected altered mental status?

options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in altered mental status. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of altered mental status. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing altered mental status, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
